10: The prayer of Jabez (1 Chronicles 4:9-10) is one of the OT’s briefest embedded prayers, set within a genealogical list without narrative context — a single pericope preserved as a liturgical or devotional tradition. The name yaʿbēṣ (Jabez) is linked to the root ʿāṣaḇ (to pain, grieve, hurt): ‘because I bore him in pain (bᵉʿōṣeḇ).’ The prayer reverses the name-content: Jabez asks that God would ‘keep me from harm/evil (rāʿāh) so that it might not bring me pain (wᵉlōʾ yiʿaṣᵉḇênî)’ — the same verbal root. The prayer thus performs a name-reversal: the man born in pain asks to be kept from pain, and God grants it. The four-part structure (bless me, enlarge my territory, let your hand be with me, keep me from harm) reflects the Israelite petition-formula pattern attested in the Psalms of lament (petition + confidence). The geographical notation of v23 (‘those who lived among plantings and in enclosures; they lived there with the king in his service’) places a group of royal craftsmen/potters in the vicinity, possibly at Kiriath-jearim/the district of Jabez (cf. Judges 1:16), where a scribal guild is mentioned in v23. The Jabez prayer preserves, embedded in genealogy, the OT’s clearest statement that individual petition within a genealogical/tribal context is legitimate and answered: YHWH responds to specific, named persons within the covenant people, not only to the nation collectively.
2: The opening of 1 Chr 5 provides the Chronicler’s explanation for an anomaly in tribal history: Reuben was Jacob’s firstborn (bᵉḵôr) but did not receive the firstborn’s double-portion inheritance. The reason given is Genesis 35:22 (Reuben’s violation of Bilhah, Jacob’s concubine) — the loss of the birthright is a consequence of moral failure. The Chronicler then separates two distinct forms of inheritance: the birthright (bᵉḵōrāh) goes to Joseph’s sons (= two tribes, Ephraim and Manasseh, the double portion), but the ruling-dignity (nāgîḏ) goes to Judah. This distinction between double-portion inheritance and governing leadership parallels ANE succession practices. In Mesopotamia, the firstborn typically received the family estate (aplum in Akkadian, comparable to bᵉḵōrāh), but kingship could pass to a non-firstborn son by divine designation or ability — Assyrian succession documents, particularly from the reigns of Sargon II and Esarhaddon, record the formal transfer of kingship to younger sons with theological legitimation. The Chronicler’s theological point is that the Davidic kingship (from Judah) is not an accident of birth-order but a divine allocation that the tribal system reflects: Reuben’s moral failure, Joseph’s double-portion, and Judah’s messianic designation all serve the same canonical structure.
1: A second Judah genealogy opens with five names — Perez, Hezron, Carmi, Hur, Shobal — as if restarting from a different archival source. The Chronicler does not harmonize this with chapter 2; he juxtaposes parallel traditions, trusting readers to understand that different genealogical registers preserved overlapping information. The repetition also serves a rhetorical function: Judah's founding figures are worth naming twice.
3: "The sons of Etam" introduces a cluster of towns in Judah's Shephelah: Jezreel, Ishma, Idbash, and Hazzelelponi (a rare feminine name in the genealogy). These "father of" formulas — where a person's name functions as a town's founding settler — are the Chronicler's standard method for encoding settlement geography within clan genealogy. The Shephelah towns listed here formed part of the agricultural heartland of Judah before and after exile.
4: Penuel as "father of Gedor" and Ezer as "father of Hushah" continue the settlement-genealogy pattern. Hur, "firstborn of Ephrathah, father of Bethlehem," anchors the genealogy back to the Calebite-Ephrathah cluster of chapter 2. The repetition of Hur here creates a genealogical web rather than a simple linear descent, reflecting the complex interconnections of Judahite clan memory.
5: Ashhur "father of Tekoa" (mentioned first in 2:24 as Hezron's posthumous son) had two wives: Helah and Naarah. The two-wife formula generates two parallel genealogical branches from a single founding figure — a narrative device that explains why certain regions showed two distinct clan traditions tracing back to the same ancestor.
6: Naarah bore Ahuzzam, Hepher, Temeni, and Haahashtari. These four sons of Naarah represent sub-clans of the Tekoa region. Hepher appears elsewhere as the name of a Manassite clan (Numbers 26:32–33) and a Canaanite city-king (Joshua 12:17), reflecting the common naming phenomenon where identical names appear in different tribal contexts — either through coincidence or through clan intermarriage that spread names across tribal boundaries.
7: Helah bore Zereth, Izhar, and Ethnan — three more sub-clans of the Ashhur genealogy. The careful preservation of both wife-lines suggests these clans maintained distinct identities into the post-exilic period, claiming descent from different Ashhur-line ancestors as a way of differentiating their territorial claims and priestly/Levitical privileges.
8: Koz fathered Anub, Zobebah, and the clans of Aharhel son of Harum. The phrase "clans of Aharhel" (misphᵉḥôt ʾaḥarḥēl) indicates that Aharhel was a significant enough ancestor to have a named clan group — a misphᵉḥāh — which was the intermediate social unit between the extended family and the tribe. Such clan names were the primary administrative unit in Persian-period Yehud's demographic records.
9: Jabez is introduced with an unusual editorial comment: "he was more honored than his brothers." In a sea of bare names, this moral evaluation stands out. The Chronicler (or his source) felt compelled to explain why Jabez merited special treatment — and the explanation will come in v10. The contrast between his name ("pain") and his honored status sets up the prayer's power: a man born under a negative omen overcame it through direct appeal to YHWH.
11: Chelub, brother of Shuhah, fathered Mehir who was "father of Eshton." The Chelubai/Caleb names that recur in these Judahite genealogies reflect either the fame of the Caleb tradition (causing multiple unrelated families to claim Calebite descent) or genuine genealogical branching where Caleb's name was common enough to generate confusion across generations.
12: Eshton fathered Beth-rapha, Paseah, and Tehinnah — Tehinnah being the "father of Ir-nahash." "Ir-nahash" means "city of the serpent/copper," possibly associated with copper-smelting activities in the Arabah region south of the Dead Sea. The Kenite metalworking connections visible in chapter 2 (v55) resurface here: Judah's southern clans maintained craft traditions involving metalwork that shaped their geographic and economic identity.
13: Kenaz's sons Othniel and Seraiah connect the Calebite genealogy to the judge Othniel son of Kenaz, Caleb's younger brother (Judges 3:9–11). Othniel, the first judge of Israel and a Calebite hero, is here placed within the broader Judahite genealogical web. His inclusion affirms that Judah's covenant story included not just kings but deliverers — the same YHWH who raised up Othniel raised up David.
14: Meonothai fathered Ophrah; Seraiah fathered Joab who was "father of Ge-harashim" — "Valley of Craftsmen." This Joab (not David's general) was the founding ancestor of a craftsmen's guild town in Judah. The notice that they were craftsmen (ḥārāšîm) embedded a professional identity within the genealogical record — these families preserved their vocational heritage through the exile, returning not just as generic Israelites but as skilled artisans with a specific role in rebuilding.
15: Caleb son of Jephunneh (the spy, distinct from Caleb son of Hezron) had sons Iru, Elah, and Naam. Elah's son Kenaz connects to the broader Kenazite clan tradition. Caleb son of Jephunneh's inclusion in the Judahite genealogy confirms his full tribal membership despite his Kenazite background — the spy who trusted YHWH when all others faltered was fully incorporated into Judah's genealogical heritage.
16: Jehallelel's four sons — Ziph, Ziphah, Tiria, Asarel — anchor the Ziph wilderness region within Judah's genealogy. Ziph is significant as David's hiding place during the Saul period (1 Samuel 23:14–24; 1 Chronicles 26:1), and the Ziphite betrayal of David is the narrative dark side of this genealogical connection. The descendants of Ziph's founding ancestor were the same people who twice reported David's location to Saul.
17: Ezra's sons include Jether, Mered, Epher, and Jalon; Mered's wife Bithiah (or Bityah) is identified as Pharaoh's daughter. A daughter of Pharaoh marrying into Judah's genealogy — and the Chronicler preserves her Egyptian royal title without embarrassment — reinforces the inclusive genealogical vision of these chapters. Bithiah/Bityah means "daughter of YHWH," a Hebrew name that the Egyptian princess apparently adopted upon entering the covenant community.
18: Bithiah the daughter of Pharaoh bore Miriam, Shammai, and Ishbah (father of Eshtemoa). Eshtemoa is the Levitical city in the Judean hills where David distributed plunder after the Ziklag raid (1 Samuel 30:28). That its founding ancestor was born of an Egyptian princess further illustrates the mixed cultural origins of Judah's settlements — origins the Chronicler preserves as legitimate rather than embarrassing.
19: Sons of Hodiah's wife (sister of Naham): the father of Keilah the Garmite and Eshtemoa the Maacathite. Keilah is the town David rescued from Philistines (1 Samuel 23:1–13) — only to discover its citizens would hand him over to Saul if Saul came. The genealogy of Keilah's founding ancestor sits between genealogical lists of Judahite towns, mapping David's entire wilderness-exile geography onto ancestral territory.
20: Shimon's sons and Ishi's sons — including Zoheth and Ben-zoheth — continue the dense southern Judahite genealogy. The multiplication of names without narrative commentary characterizes this section of Chronicles as a documentary archive: names preserved not for their stories but for their genealogical function as clan-anchors for post-exilic families claiming Judahite land rights.
21: The sons of Shelah son of Judah (Judah's third son by the Canaanite Bathshua) founded linen-working guilds: Er father of Lecah, Laadah father of Mareshah, and the families of the linen workers at Beth-ashbea. The preservation of craft guild genealogies alongside tribal genealogies reflects the Chronicler's understanding that YHWH's purposes were served by skilled workers as surely as by warriors and priests.
22: Jokim, men of Cozeba, Joash, and Saraph — who ruled in Moab and returned to Lehem — with the note that "the records are ancient." The Chronicler's acknowledgment that his sources are old is a rare moment of historiographical transparency: he knows he is working from fragmentary materials and signals this explicitly rather than pretending to complete knowledge. The Moabite connection in Shelah's line echoes the Ruth narrative's Moabite strand within Judah's story.
23: "These were the potters who lived at Netaim and Gederah; they lived there in the king's service." The genealogy closes with a craftsmen's note: the Shelah clans were royal potters, producing vessels for the court and temple. This royal service connection — *ʿim-hammelek bᵉmlāʾktô* — preserved the clan's identity as skilled artisans in royal employ. Post-exilic readers who had returned to rebuild the temple would have recognized their own craft-heritage ancestors in this notice.
24: Simeon's genealogy opens with five sons: Nemuel/Jemuel, Jamin, Jarib/Jachin, Zerah/Zohar, and Shaul. The variations from Genesis 46:10 reflect divergent traditions — the Chronicler notes them without harmonizing. Simeon's territory was embedded within Judah's (Joshua 19:1–9) because Simeon's inheritance was insufficient for a full tribal settlement; genealogically and geographically, Simeon was absorbed into Judah during the monarchic period.
25: Simeon's genealogy continues: Shallum → Mibsam → Mishma. These three-generation descents track Simeonite clan development from the wilderness period into the settlement era. The compressed format — three names with no narrative — reflects how Simeon's tribal history faded from prominence once the tribe lost independent territorial status within Judah.
26: Mishma's sons: Hammuel → Zaccur → Shimei with his sixteen sons and six daughters. The Chronicler notes Shimei's large family as unusual — "but his brothers did not have many children, so their whole clan did not multiply as much as Judah." This demographic comparison acknowledges Simeon's relative smallness within the larger Judahite context, explaining the tribe's eventual absorption without attributing blame.
27: "Simeon's sons did not multiply as much as the men of Judah" — a rare demographic observation that frames Simeon's genealogy as a story of diminishment rather than growth. The reason given is simply that they had fewer children — no theological explanation is offered. The Chronicler's restraint here is notable: not every tribal diminishment requires a moral explanation; sometimes demography is simply demography.
28: Simeon's settlements: Beer-sheba, Moladah, Hazar-shual, and others listed in vv28–31 correspond closely to Joshua 19:1–8. Beer-sheba, the southernmost Judahite city and the endpoint of the "Dan to Beersheba" idiom, was a Simeonite settlement before becoming a generic symbol for Israel's geographic boundaries. The genealogy embeds this geographic knowledge within Simeonite clan memory.
29: The continuation of Simeonite settlements: Bilhah, Ezem, Tolad, Bethuel, Hormah, and Ziklag. Ziklag — the Philistine city given to David by Achish (1 Samuel 27:6) — is here identified as a Simeonite settlement, explaining why an Israelite city would have ended up in Philistine control. The post-exilic reader would have understood: Simeon's territorial vacuum in the south created the conditions for Philistine encroachment.
30: Beth-marcaboth, Hazar-susim, Beth-biri, and Shaaraim — with the note that "these were their cities until David became king." The temporal marker "until David" indicates a significant territorial reorganization: David's kingdom absorbed Simeonite settlements into the royal administrative network, ending their status as distinctly Simeonite cities. The Chronicler preserves the pre-Davidic settlement list as historical record.
31: The five additional settlements and the genealogical note that these were Simeon's cities close the geographic section. The combination of genealogy and settlement list creates a territorial claim: post-exilic Simeonites (if any remained as a distinct group) could point to this list as evidence of their ancestral claim to southern Judean towns. For the Chronicler, preserving these lists was an act of justice toward smaller, less prominent tribal groups.
32: Thirteen leaders of Simeonite clans are named as "princes in their clans" — a title reflecting the Persian-period administrative structure where clan leaders (nᵉśîʾîm) held recognized authority over family groups. The Chronicler's listing of these names suggests he had access to relatively contemporary Simeonite clan records, not just ancient genealogies.
33: The summary: these Simeonite families multiplied greatly and went to Gerar as far as the eastern side of the valley, seeking pasture. The expansion movement reflects Simeon's agro-pastoral economy: when the traditional territory became overpopulated, clans expanded outward into marginal grazing land. This pastoral pressure-valve migration is a recurring pattern in ancient Near Eastern tribal history.
34: Simeonite princes named here — Meshobab, Jamlech, Joshah, Joel, Jehu, Elioenai, Jaakobah, Jeshohaiah, Asaiah, Adiel, Jesimiel, Benaiah, Ziza — form an unusually long list of named leaders. The name density suggests the Chronicler had access to a Simeonite administrative document, perhaps from the Persian-period satrapy records, rather than ancient tribal memory alone.
35: Elioenai and others descended from "Shihi" continue the Simeonite leadership register. The names become less recognizable as they approach the Chronicler's own time — suggesting these were contemporary figures whose records were still accessible in Persian-period archives rather than names reconstructed from ancient oral tradition.
36: The continuation of Simeonite prince names through Jeshaiah and Adiel closes the leadership register. These thirteen men (or their clans) represent the organizational structure of Simeon's community within Persian-period Yehud — small enough to lose independent tribal identity but large enough to maintain a genealogical record that the Chronicler deemed worth preserving.
37: Ziza son of Shiphi through Allon to Jedaiah — a four-generation descent ending in one of the named Simeonite expansion leaders. The genealogical depth here (four generations from a named ancestor) suggests this was a recent enough event — the Hezekiah-era expansion to Gedor — that four generations of ancestry could still be verified from family memory or clan records.
38: "These mentioned by name were princes in their clans, and their fathers' houses increased greatly." The summary before the expansion narrative affirms that genealogical record-keeping and territorial expansion went together: families grew, needed land, and used their genealogical credentials to establish claims to new territory. The Chronicler presents this as legitimate and divinely providential.
39: The Simeonites sought pasture for their flocks at Gedor (possibly Gerar, the Philistine territory) near the eastern valley. The peaceful pastoral expansion narrative — seeking grazing land rather than waging war — reflects the Chronicler's understanding that post-exilic community growth happened through diligence and opportunity rather than conquest. The same model applied to the returning exiles: find good land, establish claims, build.
40: The land was spacious, quiet, and peaceful — fat pasture. The description echoes Canaan's original appeal to the patriarchs and the pre-conquest spies' reports. "The former inhabitants were Hamites" — the Chronicler identifies the pre-Israelite population, perhaps to explain the vacancy or to establish that the Simeonites were displacing a non-Israelite population rather than competing with fellow Israelites.
41: The Simeonites under Hezekiah destroyed the Hamite and Meunite settlements and settled in their place. The note that this happened "in the days of Hezekiah king of Judah" dates the expansion to the late 8th century BCE, during the same period when Hezekiah was conducting religious reform (2 Chr 29–31). Territorial expansion and covenant renewal went together in the Chronicler's historiography.
42: Five hundred Simeonites went further to Mount Seir, led by Pelatiah, Neariah, Rephaiah, and Uzziel — sons of Ishi. They struck the remaining Amalekites and "lived there to this day." The Chronicler's use of "to this day" indicates either a source document that used this phrase contemporaneously, or that some Simeonite presence in the Seir region persisted into his own era. The destruction of Amalekites resonates with the command of 1 Sam 15 — Simeon here completes what Saul failed to do.
43: "They struck the remnant of the Amalekites who had escaped, and they have lived there to this day." The Amalekites — Israel's archetypal enemy since Exodus 17 — are finally eliminated by an otherwise obscure Simeonite expedition during Hezekiah's reign. The Chronicler places this fulfillment of ancient covenant mandate within a genealogical context: even the smallest tribal groups, when faithful, could accomplish what the great king Saul could not.
